metadata.title: DENSITY OF ALGEBRAIC POINTS ON PRODUCTS OF CURVES
metadata.abstract: > Abstract. In this paper, we initiate the systematic study of density of algebraic points on surfaces. We give an effective asymptotic range in which the density degree set has regular behavior dictated by the index. By contrast, in small degree, the question of density is subtle and depends on the arithmetic of the curves. We give several explicit examples displaying these different behaviors, including products of genus 2 curves with and without dense quadratic points. These results for products of curves have applications to questions about algebraic points on closely related surfaces, such as rank growth on abelian surfaces and bielliptic surfaces.

Let _X_ be a smooth, projective, and geometrically connected scheme defined over a number field _k_ . We will refer to such a scheme as a nice variety. Write _δ_ ( _X/k_ ) for the density degree set of _X/k_ : the set of natural numbers _d_ for which the closed points of degree _d_ are Zariski dense in _X_ . We denote by N the set of all positive integers. For a nice curve _C_ of genus _g_ , the structure of the set _δ_ ( _C/k_ ) is known to reflect the geometry of _C_ . For instance, min _δ_ ( _C/k_ ) is related to the gonality of _C_ [17, Corollary 5.2.2], and 

(1) 

_δ_ ( _C/k_ ) _∩_ N _≥_ 2 _g_ = ind( _C/k_ )N _∩_ N _≥_ 2 _g,_ 

where ind( _C/k_ ) denotes the index of _C_ [17, Proposition 5.1.1]. 

Far less is known when the dimension of _X_ is at least 2. In this paper, we initiate the study of Zariski density of algebraic points on surfaces in a case that builds upon our understanding for curves. The primary focus is the product of two nice curves _X_ = _C × D_ over _k_ . The structure of _δ_ ( _C × D/k_ ) is constrained by the structures of _δ_ ( _C/k_ ) and _δ_ ( _D/k_ ): if the genus of one of _C_ or _D_ is at most 9 we show that 

(2) 

_δ_ ( _C/k_ ) _· δ_ ( _D/k_ ) _⊆ δ_ ( _C × D/k_ ) _⊆ δ_ ( _C/k_ ) _∩ δ_ ( _D/k_ ) _._ 

For curves of arbitrary genera, the upper bound still holds but the lower bound depends on degrees of the P[1] -parameterized points (see Proposition 2.13). 

Asymptotically, it is known that for any nice variety _X_ , the density degree set _δ_ ( _X/k_ ) contains all sufficiently large multiplies of ind( _X/k_ ) [17, Proposition B.0.1], though this result is not effective. Our first main result is an analogue of (1) for _X_ = _C × D_ . For simplicity, we state the result in the case that _C_ and _D_ both have a _k_ -point; the general result is Theorem 3.6. 

**Theorem 1.1** (Corollary 3.3) **.** _Let C and D be nice curves over k of genera gC and gD. Assume further that C and D both have k-rational points, then_ 


For _gC, gD ≤_ 2, we can sharpen this containment using the explicit geometry of low genus curves. As above, we state our result when _C_ and _D_ both have a _k_ -point. 

**Theorem 1.2** (Theorem 4.9, Theorem 5.8, Proposition 6.1) **.** _Suppose that C and D are nice curves of genera gC and gD and assume that C and D both have k-rational points._ 

_(i) If gC_ = _gD_ = 1 _, then_ N _\ {_ 1 _,_ 2 _} ⊆ δ_ ( _C × D/k_ ) _⊆_ N _._ 

_(ii) If gC_ = 1 _and gD_ = 2 _, then_ N _≥_ 2 _\ {_ 2 _,_ 3 _,_ 5 _,_ 7 _} ⊆ δ_ ( _C × D/k_ ) _⊆_ N _≥_ 2 _._ 

_(iii) If gC_ = _gD_ = 2 _, then_ N _≥_ 2 _\ {_ 2 _,_ 3 _,_ 5 _,_ 6 _,_ 7 _,_ 9 _,_ 11 _} ⊆ δ_ ( _C × D/k_ ) _⊆_ N _≥_ 2 _._ 1 

J. BERG, Y. FU, E. GAZAKI, M. PORZIO, J. RAWSON, AND I. VOGT 

2 

_Remark_ 1.3 _._ In each case of Theorem 1.2, the results can be sharpened further with additional assumptions on the curves. 

If _C_ and _D_ are elliptic curves, we show in Corollary 4.2 that 1 _∈ δ_ ( _C × D/k_ ) if and only if both curves have positive rank. In Theorem 4.3 we show that 2 _∈ δ_ ( _C × D/k_ ) provided, for instance, that one of _C_ or _D_ has _j_ -invariant different from 0 _,_ 1728. 

In Theorem 1.2 part (ii), if _C_ is an isogeny factor of the Jacobian of _D_ , we show in Proposition 2.15 that _δ_ ( _C × D/k_ ) = _δ_ ( _D/k_ ). Further results depend on whether _D_ has a rational Weierstrass point (see Proposition 5.7 and Theorem 5.8). 

Finally, when both curves have genus 2, our general bounds depend on the index of each of the curves and whether the corresponding product surface has index 1, 2 or 4 (cf. Proposition 6.1 and Corollary 6.3). 

To prove Theorem 1.1, we construct explicit collections of curves _Xγ_ of bounded genus in _C × D_ passing through fixed sets of closed points, and such that their union is dense in _C × D_ . Consequently, applying the bound (1) on each curve, we conclude that for any _d ≥_ 2 _g_ ( _Xγ_ ), the surface has dense degree _d_ points. To prove Theorem 1.2, we both construct lower genus families and exploit the fact that (1) can be sharpened with more explicit knowledge of the divisors on _Xγ_ . 

1.1. **Small Degree Points.** The method of covering the surface with curves and applying the previous strategy, which relies on the Riemann–Roch Theorem, is impractical or impossible for points of very small degree. Instead, we construct these points by pulling back points from other varieties, which requires proving density of rational points on surfaces. This is quite difficult and entangled with several deep conjectures in number theory. For example, we show in Theorem 5.10 that the Parity Conjecture sometimes implies that 3 _∈ δ_ ( _C × D/k_ ) when _gC_ = 1 and _gD_ = 2. 

For quadratic points on _C ×D_ with _gC, gD ≤_ 2, we consider the quotient surface _S_ := ( _C ×D_ ) _/ι_ , where _ι_ is a hyperelliptic involution. Since _C ×D → S_ is a finite map of degree 2, if 1 _̸∈ δ_ ( _C ×D/k_ ) and 1 _∈ δ_ ( _S/k_ ), then we can conclude via pullback that 2 _∈ δ_ ( _C × D/k_ ). (In fact, if Pic[0] _C_[and] Pic[0] _D_[both][have][rank][0,][the][density][of][quadratic][points][on] _[C][ ×][ D]_[can][be][reduced][to][the][density] of rational points on finitely many surfaces of the form ( _C × D_ ) _/ι_ .) 

In low genus, the surface _S_ has interesting geometry. When _C_ and _D_ are elliptic curves, _S_ is the Kummer _K_ 3 surface associated to the abelian surface _C × D_ . When _gC_ = 1 _, gD_ = 2, _S_ is a quadratic twist elliptic fibration over P[1] . Conjecturally, all such surfaces have dense rational points [5, Conjecture 1.1]. In Section 5.1 we give some examples when this conjecture can be verified. 

Lastly, when both _C_ and _D_ have genus 2, the surface _S_ is of general type, and the BombieriLang conjecture predicts that it does not have dense rational points. In Proposition 6.10, we give an example, based on an idea of Adam Logan, of genus 2 curves _C_ and _D_ with _δ_ ( _C/k_ ) = _δ_ ( _D/k_ ) = 2N for which 2 _∈/ δ_ ( _C × D/k_ ) despite the fact that there exist degree 2 points on _C × D_ . 

On the other hand, when Pic[0] _C_[or][Pic][0] _D_[has][positive][rank,][there][can][be][additional][sources][of] quadratic points. For example, we show in Proposition 6.5 that the quadratic points are dense on a self-product _C × C_ of a genus 2 curve with Pic[0] _C_[of][positive][rank][by][pulling][back][rational] points from Pic[0] _C_[.] 

Some of the arithmetic complexity is eased by considering the potential density degree set. Recall that for a variety _X/k_ , an integer _d_ is in the potential density degree set _℘_ ( _X/k_ ) if there exists a finite extension _k[′] /k_ for which _d ∈ δ_ ( _X/k[′]_ ). If _X_ = _C × D_ is a product of curves and _C_ has genus 0 or 1, we have _℘_ ( _C × D/k_ ) = _℘_ ( _D/k_ ). In the case of products of genus 2 curves, we give a nearly complete description of the potential density degree set in Theorem 6.15. 

1.2. **Applications to non-product surfaces.** One of the main reasons for studying product surfaces is their close relation to the more well-studied setting of curves. Another reason is their closeness to several other interesting families of surfaces. Our methods and results can be applied to principally polarized abelian surfaces and to biellliptic surfaces in particular. 

DENSITY OF ALGEBRAIC POINTS ON PRODUCTS OF CURVES 

3 

In both cases, we make use of the fact they are covered by a product of curves. One subtlety is that the image of a degree _d_ point under a finite map may be a smaller degree point. To circumvent this issue, we utilize constructions of points where we can guarantee that the residue field is a primitive extension of _k_ . 

**Theorem 1.4** (cf. Theorem 6.4, Theorem 7.3) **.** _If A/k is an abelian surface that is isogenous (over k) to a principally polarized abelian surface, then_ 


_If, furthermore, A is isogenous to the Jacobian of a genus_ 2 _curve C/k, then_ 2 _∈ δ_ ( _A/k_ ) _._ 

These density statements for abelian surfaces can be re-interpreted as statements about the behavior of ranks of these abelian varieties over field extensions (see Corollary 4.10). This interpretation is one of the key steps for the following description of the density degree set of a bielliptic surface. 

**Theorem 1.5** (Corollary 4.13) **.** _Let S_ = ( _E_ 1 _× E_ 2) _/G be a bielliptic surface over k, where G acts on E_ 1 _via translations by k-rational points and on E_ 2 _such that E_ 2 _/G ≃_ P[1] _. Then,_ 


_Remark_ 1.6 _._ One of the main properties of the density degree set of curves is that it is closed under multiplication by positive integers [17, Section 5.2.1]. Whether the same property holds for higher dimensional varieties is still unknown. However, in many cases that we consider, our sharper description of the density degree set implies that it satisfies this multiplicativity property. 

1.3. **Outline.** The paper is organized as follows. In Section 2, we recall what is known about the density degree sets of curves. We also prove the upper and lower bounds on the density degree set of a product surface given in (2). Section 3 is devoted to describing our general strategy and proving Theorem 1.1. Products of elliptic curves and bielliptic surfaces are discussed in Section 4. Section 5 concerns the product of an elliptic curve and a curve of genus 2. Products of two genus 2 curves, and also Jacobians of genus 2 curves are studied in Section 6. In this section we also study the potential density degree set for products of low genus curves. Section 7 extends the description of the density degree set of a principally polarized abelian surfaces via isogenies. 

1.4. **Notation.** Unless otherwise stated, throughout this paper _k_ will be a number field. All varieties will be defined over _k_ and will be assumed to be nice (i.e. smooth, projective, geometrically connected). The index of a variety _X_ will be denoted by ind( _X/k_ ). 

**Acknowledgments.** This work began at the American Institute of Mathematics workshop “Degree _d_ points on algebraic surfaces” in March 2024. We would like to thank the organizers Nathan Chen and Bianca Viray, as well as all of the staff at AIM and the National Science Foundation for their support. We would also like to thank Niven Achenjang, Adam Logan, Samir Siksek, and Rosa Winter for helpful conversations. E.G. was partially supported by NSF grant DMS-2302196. J.R. was supported by UK EPSRC grant EP/W523793/1. I.V. was supported in part by NSF grants DMS-2200655 and DMS-2338345.

We give an effective asymptotic range in which the density degree set has regular behavior dictated by the index. By contrast, in small degree, the question of density is subtle and depends on the arithmetic of the curves. We give several explicit examples displaying these different behaviors, including products of genus 2 curves with and without dense quadratic points. These results for products of curves have applications to questions about algebraic points on closely related surfaces, such as rank growth on abelian surfaces and bielliptic surfaces.

Let X be a smooth, projective, and geometrically connected scheme defined over a number field k. We will refer to such a scheme as a nice variety. Write δ(X/k) for the density degree set of X/k: the set of natural numbers d for which the closed points of degree d are Zariski dense in X. We denote by N the set of all positive integers. For a nice curve C of genus g, the structure of the set δ(C/k) is known to reflect the geometry of C. For instance, min δ(C/k) is related to the gonality of C [17, Corollary 5.2.2], and δ(C/k) ∩ N≥2g = ind(C/k)N ∩ N≥2g, where ind(C/k) denotes the index of C [17, Proposition 5.1.1]. Far less is known when the dimension of X is at least 2. In this paper, we initiate the study of Zariski density of algebraic points on surfaces in a case that builds upon our understanding for curves. The primary focus is the product of two nice curves X = C × D over k. The structure of δ(C × D/k) is constrained by the structures of δ(C/k) and δ(D/k): if the genus of one of C or D is at most 9 we show that δ(C/k) · δ(D/k) ⊆ δ(C × D/k) ⊆ δ(C/k) ∩ δ(D/k). For curves of arbitrary genera, the upper bound still holds but the lower bound depends on degrees of the P1-parameterized points (see Proposition 2.13).

evidence.original_text: Definition 2.1. Let X be a nice variety over a field k. The density degree set δ(X/k) is the set of all positive integers d such that the degree d points on X are Zariski dense. Definition 2.2. Let X be a nice variety over a field k. The potential degree set ℘(X/k) is the union of δ(X/k′) as k′/k ranges over all possible finite extensions.

entries.description: Definition of density degree set δ(X/k) and potential density degree set ℘(X/k)

evidence.original_text: The main properties of δ(C/k) that we will need in this article are the following: (1) δ(C/k) is closed under multiplication by positive integers [17, Section 5.2.1]. (2) δ(C/k) contains all multiples of the index of C that are at least max(2g, 1) [17, Proposition 5.1.1]. (3) Every closed point on C of degree at least g + 1 is P^1-parameterized [17, Corollary 4.1.2]. (4) If k′/k is a finite extension, then δ_P^1(C/k) ⊂ δ_P^1(C/k′) [17, Lemma 5.5.2].

evidence.original_text: Proposition 2.13. Let C, D be nice curves. Then δ_P^1(C/k) · δ(D/k) ⊆ δ(C × D/k). In particular, (δ(C/k) ∩ N≥gC+1) · δ(D/k) ⊆ δ(C × D/k). If gC ≤ 9, then δ(C/k) · δ(D/k) ⊆ δ(C × D/k).

evidence.original_text: Lemma 2.14. Let S be a nice surface over k. Suppose that there exists an infinite set T and nice curves Xt over k, for each t ∈ T, such that (1) for all t ∈ T, there exists a map Xt → S which is birational onto its image Im(Xt), and (2) ∪_{t∈T} Im(Xt) is not contained in a proper closed subvariety of S, and (3) d ∈ δ(Xt/k) for all t ∈ T. Then d ∈ δ(S/k).

evidence.original_text: Lemma 3.1. Let C and D be nice curves and let Z ⊂ C × D be an effective zero-cycle with projections ZC ⊂ C and ZD ⊂ D. Let fC: C → P^1 and fD: D → P^1 be nonconstant morphisms such that fC(ZC^{red}) = fD(ZD^{red}) = ∞ ∈ P^1. For any automorphism γ: P^1 → P^1 fixing ∞, the fiber product Xγ := C ×_{fC, γ∘fD} D is a curve of arithmetic genus (deg(fC)−1)(deg(fD)−1)+deg(fC)gD+deg(fD)gC. The inclusion (πC, πD): Xγ → C × D sends Xγ to a curve on C × D through the zero-cycle Z. Varying γ in any infinite family, this family of curves sweeps out a dense locus in C × D.

evidence.original_text: Let E1, . . . , En be elliptic curves over k. Then the following are equivalent: (1) d ∈ δ(E1 × · · · × En/k). (2) There exists a degree d point P ∈ E1 × · · · × En such that the image of P under each of the projections πj : E1 × · · · × En → Ej has infinite order in Ej(k(P)). Proof. We first prove (1) ⇒ (2). By the Northcott property of the canonical height, there are finitely many torsion points on Ej of degree at most d. Call the set of such points Td,j. The unionn j=1 πj−1Td,j is a Zariski closed subset of E1 × · · · × En. Thus if d ∈ δ(E1 × · · · × En/k), there exist a degree d point outside this subset, i.e., all of whose projections are nontorsion. To prove (2) ⇒ (1), let Pj := πj(P) be the jth projection and consider the subgroup B := ⟨P1⟩× · · ·×⟨Pn⟩. The union of the points of B are dense in E1 ×· · ·×En. Furthermore, every point in B is defined over k(P) since each Pj is defined over k(P). Thus P ∈ B ⊂ (E1 ×· · ·×En)(k(P)). For each of the (finitely many) proper subextensions k ⊆ ki ⊊ k(P), let Hi := B ∩ (E1 ×· · ·× En)(ki). Then each Hi is a subgroup of B and P ̸∈iHi. Thus by [17, Lemma 4.3.8], there exists a finite index subgroup H ⊂ B such that (P + H) ∩ Hi = ∅ for all i. Since H is finite-index in B, the union of the points of P + H is also dense in X. We have arranged that for each Q ∈ P + H, the residue field is exactly k(P), which is a degree d extension of k. Thus d ∈ δ(E1 ×· · ·× En/k).

evidence.original_text: The only case left in question is when rk(E1(k)) = rk(E2(k)) = 0 and both elliptic curves have j-invariant 0 or 1728. By Lemma 4.1, it suffices to find a quadratic extension L/k over which both curves have positive rank. Let L/k be a quadratic extension in which all places of bad reduction of E1 and E2 are split, and exactly one real place of k is not split; such an extension exists since it is specified by finitely many local conditions. Over L, both the root numbers of E1 and E2 are negative. Indeed, the product of the local root numbers for the finite places is 1 since W(E/K p1) = W(E/K p2) for each place p1, p2 lying above a place of bad reduction p, and there are an odd number of archimedean places by construction. Thus, by the Parity Conjecture, both E1 and E2 have positive rank over L.

Let D be the normalization of the projective closure of y12= g4(x1). Let E := Pic0D, then 2 ∈ δ(E × C/k). Proof. Let X be the fiber product of C and D over P1 via the maps x1 and x2. This has equations y12= g4(x)g2(x)and y22= g4(x). We can replace the first by y32= g2(x), where y1= y2y3. That is, we can write X = {(x, y2, y3) : y22= g4(x), y32= g2(x)}. Since g2 is monic, the curve y32= g2(x) is a pointed conic, and so isomorphic to P1. Thus, the degree 2 map X → P1, (x, y2, y3) → (x, y3) shows that X is a hyperelliptic curve and 2 ∈ δ(X/k). On the other hand, the degree 2 projection X → D implies that E is an isogeny factor of Pic0X, and so 2 ∈ δ(X × E/k) by Proposition 2.15. The covering map X → C induces a covering E × X → E × C. Since 1 ∈/ δ(E × C/k), the image of the degree 2 points on E × X must yield a Zariski dense set of degree 2 points on E × C.

evidence.original_text: Assume the Parity Conjecture. Let E/Q be an elliptic curve with split semistable reduction. Suppose that C is a nice genus 2 curve with integral model C with good reduction at the bad primes of E. Moreover, assume that (1) for each finite prime p of bad reduction of E, |Cp(Fp)| < p + 1 or |Cp(Fp)| ≥ p + 10, (2) there exists a rational map f : C  P1Z over Z, of degree 3, such that it is defined at each bad prime of E and at least one R-fiber is not totally split. Then 3 ∈ δ(E × C/Q). Proof. If the number of primes of bad reduction is even, then, since uQ = 1, we have w(E/Q) = −1 and so rk E(Q) > 0 and the result follows since 3 ∈ δ(C/Q). We therefore assume there are an odd number of such primes of bad reduction. Our approach will be to find points t ∈ P1Q such that the fiber f−1(t) is a degree 3 point with residue field K and E(K) has positive rank. Let p be a prime of bad reduction of E. By assumption, either In the first case, f|Fp cannot be surjective on Fp rational points, and so there exists tp ∈ P1(Fp), such that f−1(tp) is a degree 3 point over Fp. In the second case, since f has at most 8 Fp-rational branch points by the Riemann-Hurwitz formula, there exists tp ∈ P1(Fp), outside of the branch locus, such that #f−1(tp)(Fp) ≥ 2. Since f has degree 3, this implies #f−1(tp)(Fp) = 3. In particular, if t ∈ Q reduces to tp modulo p, then in the first case, p will be totally inert in K, and in the second, p will be totally split at K. We also have a fiber, tR, such that the fiber of f above tR consists of a real point and a complex-conjugate pair. For t sufficiently near tR, this will imply K has a real and complex embedding. By weak approximation, there exist infinitely many Q-rational points in P1Q which restrict to tp for each bad prime p, and sufficiently close to tR. For each such t, E has positive rank over K by (6) and the Parity Conjecture, since E/k has an odd number of finite places of bad reduction, and an even number of infinite places. In particular, f−1(t) × E ⊂ C × E has dense K-rational points. Repeating this for the infinitely many suitable t gives the density of degree 3 points.

If P ∈ C is a point of degree 3, then it is P1-parameterized by property (3), and 3 is in δ(C/k). In the case when ind(C/k) = 1 but there is no degree 3 point, and so 3 ∉ δ(C/k), let γ be a 0-cycle on C of degree 1. By the Riemann–Roch theorem, γ + KC is equivalent to an effective 0-cycle γ′ of degree 3. Since C has no degree 3 point, the cycle γ′ contains a k-point.
In particular, since the gonality of a pointed curve of genus g is at most g + 1, we have N≥2(2(gC + 1)(gD + 1) + (gC + 1)gD + (gD + 1)gC) ⊂ δ(C × D/k).
The fiber product Xγ := C ×γ◦fC, fD D is a curve of arithmetic genus (deg(fC) − 1)(deg(fD) − 1) + deg(fC)gD + deg(fD)gC.

evidence.original_text: The curve C/k has infinitely many points of degree d if and only if it has a point of degree d that is either P1- or AV-parameterized [17, Theorem 4.4.3]. Consequently, the density degree set δ(C/k) decomposes δ(C/k) = δP1(C/k) ∪ δAV(C/k), where δAV(C/k) is the set of degrees of AV-parameterized points[17, Corollary 5.0.1, Definition 5.0.2]. The main properties of δ(C/k) that we will need in this article are the following: (1) δ(C/k) is closed under multiplication by positive integers [17, Section 5.2.1]. (2) δ(C/k) contains all multiples of the index of C that are at least max(2g, 1) [17, Proposition 5.1.1]. (3) Every closed point on C of degree at least g + 1 is P1-parameterized [17, Corollary 4.1.2]. (4) If k′/k is a finite extension, then δP1(C/k) ⊂ δP1(C/k′) [17, Lemma 5.5.2].

The structure of δ(C × D/k) is constrained by the structures of δ(C/k) and δ(D/k): if the genus of one of C or D is at most 9 we show that δ(C/k) · δ(D/k) ⊆ δ(C × D/k) ⊆ δ(C/k) ∩ δ(D/k). For curves of arbitrary genera, the upper bound still holds but the lower bound depends on degrees of the P1-parameterized points (see Proposition 2.13).

If E1, E2 are isogenous over k, it follows immediately by Proposition 2.15 that δ(E1 × E2/k) = δ(E1/k) = δ(E2/k). Using the group structure of elliptic curves, the density degree set of an arbitrary product of elliptic curves admits the following more straightforward description. Lemma 4.1. Let E1, . . . , En be elliptic curves over k. Then the following are equivalent: (1) d ∈ δ(E1 × · · · × En/k). (2) There exists a degree d point P ∈ E1 × · · · × En such that the image of P under each of the projections πj : E1 × · · · × En → Ej has infinite order in Ej(k(P)).

evidence.section: Products of an elliptic curve and genus 2 curve
evidence.original_text: If E has positive rank, the lower bound of Proposition 2.13 coincides with the upper bound, hence δ(C × E/k) = δ(C/k). This leaves the rank zero case, in which case the two bounds do not coincide.

Proposition 6.5. Let C be a nice genus 2 curve over k. Let Pic0C be the Jacobian of C. Then δ(Pic0C/k) ⊆ δ(C × C/k) ⊆ 2δ(Pic0C/k). Proof. Since gC = 2, the Jacobian Pic0C is an abelian surface. Similarly to the proof of Theorem 6.4, we consider the birational morphism σ : Sym2(C) → Pic0C. Precomposing with the canonical map C × C → Sym2(C) gives a morphism C × C → Pic0C, and there exists a nonempty open subset U ⊂ C × C such that the restriction U → σ(U) is a finite morphism of degree 2. Let d ∈ δ(Pic0C/k). Then d ∈ δ(σ(U)/k), and every degree d point of σ(U) pulls back to a point of U of degree either d or 2d. If a dense subset of the preimages have degree 2d, we are done. Otherwise, we conclude that d ∈ δ(C × C/k) and in particular d ∈ δ(C/k). Since C is hyperelliptic, 2 ∈ δ(C/k), and hence the lower bound δ(C/k) · δ(C/k) ⊆ δ(C × C/k) guarantees that 2d ∈ δ(C × C/k). Now if Pic0C is simple and rk Pic0C(k) > 0, it follows by Proposition 7.1 that 1 ∈ δ(Pic0C/k), and hence 2 ∈ δ(C × C/k).

In this paper, we initiate the systematic study of density of algebraic points on surfaces. We give an effective asymptotic range in which the density degree set has regular behavior dictated by the index. By contrast, in small degree, the question of density is subtle and depends on the arithmetic of the curves. We give several explicit examples displaying these different behaviors, including products of genus 2 curves with and without dense quadratic points. These results for products of curves have applications to questions about algebraic points on closely related surfaces, such as rank growth on abelian surfaces and bielliptic surfaces.

The primary focus is the product of two nice curves X = C × D over k. The structure of δ(C × D/k) is constrained by the structures of δ(C/k) and δ(D/k): if the genus of one of C or D is at most 9 we show that δ(C/k) · δ(D/k) ⊆ δ(C × D/k) ⊆ δ(C/k) ∩ δ(D/k). For curves of arbitrary genera, the upper bound still holds but the lower bound depends on degrees of the P1-parameterized points (see Proposition 2.13). Asymptotically, it is known that for any nice variety X, the density degree set δ(X/k) contains all sufficiently large multiplies of ind(X/k) [17, Proposition B.0.1], though this result is not effective. Our first main result is an analogue of (1) for X = C × D. For simplicity, we state the result in the case that C and D both have a k-point; the general result is Theorem 3.6. Theorem 1.1 (Corollary 3.3). Let C and D be nice curves over k of genera gC and gD. Assume further that C and D both have k-rational points, then δ(C × D/k) ∩ N≥N(C,D) = ind(C × D/k)N ∩ N≥N(C,D), where N(C, D) = 2((gon(C) − 1)(gon(D) − 1) + gon(C)gD + gon(D)gC). For gC, gD ≤ 2, we can sharpen this containment using the explicit geometry of low genus curves. As above, we state our result when C and D both have a k-point. Theorem 1.2 (Theorem 4.9, Theorem 5.8, Proposition 6.1). Suppose that C and D are nice curves of genera gC and gD and assume that C and D both have k-rational points. (i) If gC = gD = 1, then N \ {1, 2} ⊆ δ(C × D/k) ⊆ N. (ii) If gC = 1 and gD = 2, then N≥2 \ {2, 3, 5, 7} ⊆ δ(C × D/k) ⊆ N≥2. (iii) If gC = gD = 2, then N≥2 \ {2, 3, 5, 6, 7, 9, 11} ⊆ δ(C × D/k) ⊆ N≥2.

If C and D are elliptic curves, we show in Corollary 4.2 that 1 ∈ δ(C × D/k) if and only if both curves have positive rank. In Theorem 4.3 we show that 2 ∈ δ(C × D/k) provided, for instance, that one of C or D has j-invariant different from 0, 1728. In Theorem 1.2 part (ii), if C is an isogeny factor of the Jacobian of D, we show in Proposition 2.15 that δ(C × D/k) = δ(D/k). Further results depend on whether D has a rational Weierstrass point (see Proposition 5.7 and Theorem 5.8). Finally, when both curves have genus 2, our general bounds depend on the index of each of the curves and whether the corresponding product surface has index 1, 2 or 4 (cf. Proposition 6.1 and Corollary 6.3).

To prove Theorem 1.1, we construct explicit collections of curves Xγ of bounded genus in C × D passing through fixed sets of closed points, and such that their union is dense in C × D. Consequently, applying the bound (1) on each curve, we conclude that for any d ≥ 2g(Xγ), the surface has dense degree d points. To prove Theorem 1.2, we both construct lower genus families and exploit the fact that (1) can be sharpened with more explicit knowledge of the divisors on Xγ.

For quadratic points on C × D with gC, gD ≤ 2, we consider the quotient surface S := (C × D)/ι, where ι is a hyperelliptic involution. Since C × D → S is a finite map of degree 2, if 1 ∉ δ(C × D/k) and 1 ∈ δ(S/k), then we can conclude via pullback that 2 ∈ δ(C × D/k). (In fact, if Pic0C and Pic0D both have rank 0, the density of quadratic points on C × D can be reduced to the density of rational points on finitely many surfaces of the form (C × D)/ι.) In low genus, the surface S has interesting geometry. When C and D are elliptic curves, S is the Kummer K3 surface associated to the abelian surface C × D. When gC = 1, gD = 2, S is a quadratic twist elliptic fibration over P1. Conjecturally, all such surfaces have dense rational points [5, Conjecture 1.1]. In Section 5.1 we give some examples when this conjecture can be verified. Lastly, when both C and D have genus 2, the surface S is of general type, and the Bombieri-Lang conjecture predicts that it does not have dense rational points. In Proposition 6.10, we give an example, based on an idea of Adam Logan, of genus 2 curves C and D with δ(C/k) = δ(D/k) = 2N for which 2 ∈/ δ(C × D/k) despite the fact that there exist degree 2 points on C × D. On the other hand, when Pic0C or Pic0D has positive rank, there can be additional sources of quadratic points. For example, we show in Proposition 6.5 that the quadratic points are dense on a self-product C × C of a genus 2 curve with Pic0C of positive rank by pulling back rational points from Pic0C.

Some of the arithmetic complexity is eased by considering the potential density degree set. Recall that for a variety X/k, an integer d is in the potential density degree set ℘(X/k) if there exists a finite extension k′/k for which d ∈ δ(X/k′). If X = C × D is a product of curves and C has genus 0 or 1, we have ℘(C × D/k) = ℘(D/k). In the case of products of genus 2 curves, we give a nearly complete description of the potential density degree set in Theorem 6.15.

One of the main reasons for studying product surfaces is their close relation to the more well-studied setting of curves. Another reason is their closeness to several other interesting families of surfaces. Our methods and results can be applied to principally polarized abelian surfaces and to biellliptic surfaces in particular. In both cases, we make use of the fact they are covered by a product of curves. One subtlety is that the image of a degree d point under a finite map may be a smaller degree point. To circumvent this issue, we utilize constructions of points where we can guarantee that the residue field is a primitive extension of k. Theorem 1.4 (cf. Theorem 6.4, Theorem 7.3). If A/k is an abelian surface that is isogenous (over k) to a principally polarized abelian surface, then δ(A/k) ∩ N≥N(A) = ind(A/k)N ∩ N≥N(A). If, furthermore, A is isogenous to the Jacobian of a genus 2 curve C/k, then 2 ∈ δ(A/k). These density statements for abelian surfaces can be re-interpreted as statements about the behavior of ranks of these abelian varieties over field extensions (see Corollary 4.10). This interpretation is one of the key steps for the following description of the density degree set of a bielliptic surface. Theorem 1.5 (Corollary 4.13). Let S = (E1 × E2)/G be a bielliptic surface over k, where G acts on E1 via translations by k-rational points and on E2 such that E2/G ≃ P1. Then, δ(S/k) ⊆ δ(E1/k) and N≥3 ∪ N≥5 ⊆ δ(S/k).

One of the main properties of the density degree set of curves is that it is closed under multiplication by positive integers [17, Section 5.2.1]. Whether the same property holds for higher dimensional varieties is still unknown. However, in many cases that we consider, our sharper description of the density degree set implies that it satisfies this multiplicativity property.

Lemma 2.3. Let C be a nice genus g curve over k. (1) If g = 1, then δ(C/k) ∩ N≥2 = (ind(C/k)N) ∩ N≥2, and 1 ∈ δ(C/k) if and only if C(k) is infinite. In particular, if C is an elliptic curve, then N≥2 ⊆ δ(C/k), with equality if and only if rk C(k) = 0. (2) If g = 2, then δ(C/k) ∩ N≥4 = (ind(C/k)N) ∩ N≥4, and δ(C/k) = {2} ∪ (ind(C/k)N) ∩ N≥4, or δ(C/k) = {2, 3} ∪ (ind(C/k)N) ∩ N≥4. Moreover, if C has index 1 but has no degree 3 points (namely the third case), then C(k) ≠ ∅.
Lemma 2.4. Let C be a non-hyperelliptic curve of genus g ≥ 3 with index 1. (1) If C(k) ≠ ∅, then 2g − 3 ∈ δ(C/k). (2) If #C(k) ≥ 2 or C(k) = ∅, then 2g − 1 ∈ δ(C/k).

Lemma 2.5. Let C1 and C2 be curves of genus g1 and g2 respectively, and assume there exists a map ϕ: C1 → C2 of degree n. Let d be an integer with (n−1)d < g1 − ng2 and d ≤ g1 − 2. If there exists an effective divisor Z1 on C1 of degree d, such that for all points P ∈ C1(k¯), and all effective divisors Z2 on C2 with h0(Z2) ≥ 2, Z1 + P − ϕ∗Z2 is not effective, then 2g1 − 2 − d ∈ δ(C1/k).

Lemma 2.7. Suppose that C/k admits a degree d divisor D whose complete linear system |D| is birationally very ample. Then C has a dense set of degree d points P ∈ C, such that |P| = |D| and k(P)/k is a degree d extension with Galois group of the Galois closure equal to Sd.

Lemma 2.9. Let E/k be an elliptic curve defined over a number field. Then for every integer d ≥ 2 there exists an extension L/k with [L : k] = d with Galois group (of the Galois closure) equal to Sd such that rk E(L) > rk E(k).
Corollary 2.10. Let E/k be an elliptic curve defined over a number field k. For each d ≥ 2, there exist infinitely many degree d points P ∈ E of infinite order such that k([m]P) = k(P) for all m ∈ Z.
Lemma 2.11. Let X be a nice surface over k, C a nice curve over k and π: X → C a surjective map between them. Then δ(X/k) ⊆ δ(C/k) and ℘(X/k) ⊆ ℘(C/k).
Corollary 2.12. Let C, D be nice curves. Then δ(C × D/k) ⊆ δ(C/k) ∩ δ(D/k).
Proposition 2.13. Let C, D be nice curves. Then δP1(C/k) · δ(D/k) ⊆ δ(C × D/k). In particular, (δ(C/k) ∩ N≥gC+1) · δ(D/k) ⊆ δ(C × D/k). If gC ≤ 9, then δ(C/k) · δ(D/k) ⊆ δ(C × D/k).

Lemma 2.14. Let S be a nice surface over k. Suppose that there exists an infinite set T and nice curves Xt over k, for each t ∈ T, such that (1) for all t ∈ T, there exists a map Xt → S which is birational onto its image Im(Xt), and (2) ∪t∈T Im(Xt) is not contained in a proper closed subvariety of S, and (3) d ∈ δ(Xt/k) for all t ∈ T. Then d ∈ δ(S/k).
Proposition 2.15. Let C be a curve of genus g ≥ 1 and E an elliptic curve over k. Assume that E is an isogeny factor of Pic0C. Then δ(C × E/k) = δ(C/k).

Lemma 3.1. Let C and D be nice curves and let Z ⊂ C × D be an effective zero-cycle with projections ZC ⊂ C and ZD ⊂ D. Let fC: C → P1 and fD: D → P1 be nonconstant morphisms such that fC(ZC red) = fD(ZD red) = ∞∈ P1. For any automorphism γ: P1 → P1 fixing ∞, the fiber product Xγ := C ×γ◦fC,fD D is a curve of arithmetic genus (deg(fC) − 1)(deg(fD) − 1)+deg(fC)gD+deg(fD)gC. The inclusion (πC, πD): Xγ → C × D sends Xγ to a curve on C × D through the zero-cycle Z. Varying γ in any infinite family, this family of curves sweeps out a dense locus in C × D.

Lemma 3.2. Let K/k be a finite extension. Suppose that P ∈ C(K) is a ramification point of fC and Q ∈ D is a ramification point of fD and fC(P) = fD(Q) = ∞. Assume that either The ramification indices of fC at P and fD at Q are relatively prime; or The points P and Q are k-rational (i.e., K = k). Then there exist infinitely many distinct automorphisms γ: P1k → P1k fixing ∞ such that the normalization Xγν of Xγ has a K-rational point over (P, Q).

Corollary 3.3. Suppose that gC ≥ 1 and gD ≥ 1 and that C(k) = ∅ and D(k) = ∅. Let N(C, D) = 2((gon(C) − 1)(gon(D) − 1) + gon(C)gD + gon(D)gC). Then δ(C × D/k) ∩ N≥N(C,D) = ind(C × D/k)N ∩ N≥N(C,D). In particular, since the gonality of a pointed curve of genus g is at most g + 1, we have δ(C × D/k) ∩ N≥2((gC+1)(gD+1)+gCgD+gDgC) = ind(C × D/k)N ∩ N≥2((gC+1)(gD+1)+gCgD+gDgC).

Corollary 3.4. Let C be an index one curve, and D a pointed curve. Suppose that C and D admit morphisms to P1 of degrees dC and dD respectively, with dC coprime to 2dD(gC − 1) (this is possible since C has index 1). Set N(C, D) = 2 ((dC − 1)(dD − 1) + dCgD + dDgC), then δ(C × D/k) ∩ N≥N(C,D) = ind(C × D/k)N ∩ N≥N(C,D).
Proposition 3.6. Let e = eff-ind(C × D/k) and let N(C, D, e) = 2((e + 1)2 + 2gC(e + 1) + 2gD(e + 1) + 2gCgD). Then δ(C × D/k) ∩ N≥N(C,D,e) = ind(C × D/k)N ∩ N≥N(C,D,e).

Lemma 3.8. Suppose that ind(C/k) = 1. Then there exists a zero cycle Z ⊂ C × D with deg(Z) = ind(C × D/k) and deg |Z| ≤ 4gCgD + (4gC + 2)(ind(D/k) − 1) + 2gC ind(D/k) + 2gD + ind(D/k). In particular, if ind(C × D/k) = 1, then ind(D/k) = 1 and deg |Z| ≤ 4gCgD + 2gC + 2gD + 1.

Lemma 4.1. Let E1, . . . , En be elliptic curves over k. Then the following are equivalent: (1) d ∈ δ(E1 × · · · × En/k). (2) There exists a degree d point P ∈ E1 × · · · × En such that the image of P under each of the projections πj: E1 × · · · × En → Ej has infinite order in Ej(k(P)).
Corollary 4.2. Let E1 and E2 be elliptic curves over k. If rk E2(k) > 0, then δ(E1 × E2/k) = δ(E1/k). In particular, 1 ∈ δ(E1 × E2/k) if and only if both rk E1(k) and rk E2(k) are positive.
Theorem 4.3. For any elliptic curves E1, E2, with j-invariants not both 0 or both 1728, 2 ∈ δ(E1 × E2/k). If Ei[2] ⊂ Ei(k), the assumption on the j-invariants can be dropped.
Corollary 4.5. In both cases considered in Theorem 4.3 there exists a quadratic field over which both elliptic curves have an increase in rank unconditionally on the ranks of E1(k), E2(k).
Proposition 4.6. Under the Parity Conjecture, 2 ∈ δ(E1 × E2/k) for any elliptic curves E1 and E2 over a number field k with a real embedding.

Theorem 4.9. For any pair of elliptic curves, E1, E2, we have 3, 5, 7 ∈ δ(E1 × E2/k). Hence N≥3 ⊆ δ(E1 × E2/k). Moreover, for all d ≥ 6, the surface E1 × E2 admits dense degree d points with Galois group (of the Galois closure) Sd.

Corollary 4.10. For any elliptic curves, E1, E2, defined over a number field, k, and d ≥ 3, there exists an extension, L, of k of degree d such that rk Ei(L) ≥ rk Ei(k) + 1 for i = 1, 2. Moreover, if d ≥ 6, the Galois group of L/k can be assumed to be Sd.

Corollary 4.11. For any elliptic curves E1, E2 over a number field k and for every d ∈ {3}∪ N≥5 there exists a degree d point (P1, P2) ∈ E1 × E2 such that each Pi is nontorsion, of degree d, and for every n ≥ 1, k(Pi) = k([n]Pi).

Corollary 4.13. Let X = (E1 × E2)/G be a bielliptic surface over k as above. Then we have containments δ(X/k) ⊆ δ(E1/k) and N≥3 ∪ N≥5 ⊆ δ(X/k). Moreover, if any of the assumptions of Theorem 4.3 is true, then 2 ∈ δ(X/k) as well.
Proposition 4.14. There are examples of bielliptic surfaces X for which 1 ∉ δ(X/k), although rk(E1(k)) > 0.

evidence.original_text: Theorem 5.10. Assume the Parity Conjecture. Let E/ Q be an elliptic curve with split semistable reduction. Suppose that C is a nice genus 2 curve with integral model C with good reduction at the bad primes of E. Moreover, assume that (1) for each finite prime p of bad reduction of E, |Cp(Fp)| < p + 1 or |Cp(Fp)| ≥ p + 10, (2) there exists a rational map f: C  P1Z over Z, of degree 3, such that it is defined at each bad prime of E and at least one R-fiber is not totally split. Then 3 ∈ δ(E × C/ Q).
Proposition 6.1. Let C, D be two nice curves of genus 2 with ind(C/k) = ind(D/k) = 1. Then, according to the arithmetic of C and D, the density set δ(C × D/k) contains the sets listed in Table 1.

Lemma 6.2. Let C, D be two nice genus 2 curves with ind(C/k) = 2. (1) If ind(D/k) = 1, then 2N \ {2, 6} ⊆ δ(C × D/k). Moreover, if 3 ∈ δ(D/k) then 6 ∈ δ(C × D/k). (2) If ind(D/k) = 2 and ind(C × D/k) = 2, then 4N ∪ 2N≥(e+3)2 ⊆ δ(C × D/k), where e is the effective index. (3) If ind(D/k) = 2 and ind(C × D/k) = 4, then δ(C × D/k) = 4N.
Corollary 6.3. Let C, D be two nice genus 2 curves with ind(C/k) = ind(D/k) = 2 and suppose that C × D has a degree 2 point. Then δ(C × D/k) ∩ N≥10 = 2N ∩ N≥10.
